old_claim.note: Industrial Standards & Testing Laboratories provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The scoped node is printed trade literature; printing is the production medium for catalogs issued by manufacturers to advertise and sell products.

source_shape.source_locator: Smithsonian defines trade literature as printed or handwritten lists of items offered for sale and says manufacturers issued trade catalogs to promote and sell products.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Smithsonian defines trade literature as business merchandising catalogs and publications, including printed lists of items for sale and manufacturer-issued trade catalogs.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because this node is specifically scoped to printed catalogs, the printing substrate belongs on the direct edge; laboratory testing is a separate standards activity not named by the catalog source.

ontology_before: Industrial Parts Catalogs depended on Industrial Standards & Testing Laboratories, making product-testing institutions look like a direct prerequisite for printed sales literature.
ontology_after: Industrial Parts Catalogs depends on Printing Press for the printed trade-literature medium, while laboratory testing is no longer a direct prerequisite.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: industrial_parts_catalogs now depends on printing_press and no longer depends on industrial_laboratories_standard_testing.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to laboratory-issued product certification catalogues rather than manufacturer trade literature.
A source showed early industrial parts catalogs directly required testing laboratories before printed trade literature could exist.
The printing_press node were narrowed away from producing printed business literature.
short_reason: Printed trade catalogs require printing; testing laboratories were the wrong prerequisite level.
